Hi Jörg,

> On 15 Jul 2016, at 17:31, Jörg Hälker <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> 
> This is more a question than an issue. I set up a cluster and I am experiencing error messages at the first start, that I can not interpret. Nevertheless it seems like it is working, just want to be sure if I understood something wrong.
> 
> The first logging is from the leader, the second logging is from a follower that gets initially initialized from the leader: Whats about this "cp: cannot stat '/var/lib/postgresql/9.5/wal_archive/00000002.history': No such file or directory" messages?
> 
> /var/lib/postgresql/9.5/wal_archive is a NFS share.
> /var/lib/postgresql/9.5/cluster is a local directory.
> 
> 

This is normal (and in fact, comes from PostgreSQL executing your recovery command):

"Normally, recovery will proceed through all available WAL segments, thereby restoring the database to the current point in time (or as close as possible given the available WAL segments). Therefore, a normal recovery will end with a "file not found" message, the exact text of the error message depending upon your choice of restore_command. You may also see an error message at the start of recovery for a file named something like 00000001.history. This is also normal and does not indicate a problem in simple recovery situations; see Section 24.3.5 for discussion."

https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/static/continuous-archiving.html
en_US.utf8

> For example if we are doing "reinitialize" or "restart", we should not run usual decision tree, because Patroni will notice that Postgres is not running and will try to start it in read-only mode.

Hmmmm.  Is it possible for those operations to actually get interrupted by a separate Patroni thread?

> It is not always the case. Patroni starts first and only then it is starting up Postgres. And actually Postgres may already be running even before Patroni has started.

Again, it depends on what you're trying to accomplish; my thinking was to use "shutdown" for STONITH.  The simplest way to do this is to have a shutdown command which shuts down postgres and patroni.  If having a general start/stop is useful, then we'll need to also add a value to the SIS key for the server which is "donotstart", and gets checked by Patroni.  Seems reasonable enough.

> If I understand you correctly you are trying to find the solution for the case when you are messed up with configuration and Postgres didn't started but initialize key remains in a configuration store forever. It will make impossible to "bootstrap" cluster with the same name once again. I guess "patroni-cli" should allow to create/remove arbitrary keys in a configuration store. Removal of keys is not really dangerous and the healthy cluster can survive it even without restarting in read-only. Basically healthy cluster will "restore" all keys in a few seconds.

Hmmm.  I'm not sure I want to go there with the CLI; it seems like encouraging people to edit keys by hand, and that's not such a good idea.  Heck, they can do that anyway with curl. For now, let's just make it so that reinitialize only works on replicas.

After giving this a thought, I'd like to propose another approach.  As you can load several `CartoContext` within the same notebook, instead of creating a new API for example data, I'd consider having a example `CartoContext`. Example:

```python
cc1 = cartoframes.CartoContext(base_url='http://cdb.localhost.lan:8888/', api_key='wadus')
cc2 = cartoframes.Examples
df = cc2.read('my_cities')
cc1.write(df, 'example_cities')
```

This way there's no need for a new API. This is better for maintenance purposes and also for learning, as users don't need to learn an specific API and can take full advantage of `CartoContext` API (in my example, `cartoframes.Examples` is a `CartoContext`itself). Internally, `cartoframes.Examples` is just a hardcoded user.

What do you think. @andy-esch ?

PS: as `Examples` should be a read-only user, the `api_key` shouldn't be the normal one. CARTO Frames should support skipping the `api_key` parameter so write permission is not granted. In a few weeks it should use the new Auth API, because it adds support for a public API key.

Some example of the improvements in access time that can be archived by delaying the loading of field data until accessing the 'data' key of the field dictionary when only a single field is to be examined:

### NEXRAD Level II Archive files:

``` Python
In [6]: %timeit radar = pyart.io.read_nexrad_archive('KATX20130717_195021_V06', delay_field_loading=False); radar.fields['reflectivity']['data']
1 loops, best of 3: 1.96 s per loop

In [7]: %timeit radar = pyart.io.read_nexrad_archive('KATX20130717_195021_V06', delay_field_loading=True); radar.fields['reflectivity']['data']
1 loops, best of 3: 943 ms per loop
```

### CfRadial

``` Python
In [8]: %timeit radar = pyart.io.read_cfradial('sgpxsaprrhicmacI5.c0.20110524.015604_NC4.nc', delay_field_loading=False); radar.fields['reflectivity_horizontal']['data']
1 loops, best of 3: 335 ms per loop

In [9]: %timeit radar = pyart.io.read_cfradial('sgpxsaprrhicmacI5.c0.20110524.015604_NC4.nc', delay_field_loading=True); radar.fields['reflectivity_horizontal']['data']
10 loops, best of 3: 57.2 ms per loop
```

Results will vary greatly depending on file content (greater speed ups when the file contains many fields) and hardware especially hard drive speed.  These tests were run on a machine with a non-SSD hard drive.

@tseaver To make the conditional definition comments a bit more clear let me describe what I have in mind (as a parallel to [`oauth2client.crypt`][1]).

1.  You implement everything in `gcloud.pubsub._gax` at the top-level, allowing for import failures.
1.  In the place where you use it (I assume `gcloud.pubsub.connection`, in a future PR?), there you conditionally import:

    ```python
    try:
        from gcloud.pubsub._gax import _PublisherAPI
        ...
        _HAVE_GAX = True
    except ImportError:
        _PublisherAPI = None  # or maybe type(None) if you want it to be a class
        ...
        _HAVE_GAX = False
    ```

Having the conditional skip in `test__gax` rely on the import success also seems out of place, since we can just require the dependency in `tox` for Py2.7 and ignore it for other versions (I know the dependency is an extra right now in `setup.py`).

[1]: https://github.com/google/oauth2client/blob/e99a4ac3c5d3c2944680ea180b71a0d144d6128b/oauth2client/crypt.py#L46-L71

Started implementing this as edits to `stdio_as`, and ran into the issue that in order to restore `sys.{stderr,stdout,stdin}` after the call, they need to be of types that we can recreate. For example, if `sys.stdout` has been replaced with an arbitrary python object which may or may not have a file descriptor underneath it, as the contextmanager exits, we would need to replace that object with a new instance over its original file descriptor.

So rather than implementing this in `stdio_as`, I think my approach will be to continue to use `stdio_as` pre-fork, and then post fork re-open `stdin/stdio/stderr` pointing to `0/1/2`.

Hi @alexeib this is strange.

I don't know exactly if a big transformer with this batch size (and max sequence length) would fit on a single GPU, but the defaults should definitely fit.
Regarding the 'model', i.e. the parameters: The actual parameters are not that big (maybe a couple hundred MB). What uses most of the GPU memory is the storage for intermediate outputs in the computation graph.

Your errors sound like a problem with mxnet and/or cuda drivers. For example, changing device ids from `1` to `-1` shouldn't change anything in terms of data parallel training, but just give Sockeye the ability to acquire any available GPU instead of the second one (index 1).
Have you tried it with pip-installed mxnet (`pip install mxnet-cu90==1.2.0`)? Also, you may want to make sure to uninstall any other mxnet version (self-compiled or pip-installed) first before doing this.

Also, could you give the full stacktrace of the error in your last comment, i.e. indicating at which point in Sockeye code `asscalar()` is called?

Okay, the code below works for me.

Note the changed line `parent_elm = parent.element.body`. The paragraph elements are not the direct children of the `<w:document>` element, there is a `<w:body>` element intervening.

I encourage you to uncomment the `print(parent_elm.xml)` line and inspect the XML it produces. That may shed light on just what's happening underneath the covers here :)

----

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# encoding: utf-8

"""
Testing iter_block_items()
"""

from __future__ import (
    absolute_import, division, print_function, unicode_literals
)

from docx import Document
from docx.document import Document as _Document
from docx.oxml.text.paragraph import CT_P
from docx.oxml.table import CT_Tbl
from docx.table import _Cell, Table
from docx.text.paragraph import Paragraph


def iter_block_items(parent):
    """
    Generate a reference to each paragraph and table child within *parent*,
    in document order. Each returned value is an instance of either Table or
    Paragraph. *parent* would most commonly be a reference to a main
    Document object, but also works for a _Cell object, which itself can
    contain paragraphs and tables.
    """
    if isinstance(parent, _Document):
        parent_elm = parent.element.body
        # print(parent_elm.xml)
    elif isinstance(parent, _Cell):
        parent_elm = parent._tc
    else:
        raise ValueError("something's not right")

    for child in parent_elm.iterchildren():
        if isinstance(child, CT_P):
            yield Paragraph(child, parent)
        elif isinstance(child, CT_Tbl):
            yield Table(child, parent)


document = Document('test.docx')
for block in iter_block_items(document):
    print('found one')
    print(block.text if isinstance(block, Paragraph) else '<table>')
```

I think I prefer to document them in `__init__`. It is easier to keep the docs up to date then because the parameters are documented nearer to where they are used.  There is a configuration option in Napoleon which helps.  From the Napoleon docs:

__napoleon_include_special_with_doc__
bool, defaults to `True` – `True` to include special members (like `__membername__`) with docstrings in the documentation. `False` to fall back to Sphinx’s default behavior.

If `True` :
```python
def __str__(self):
    """
    This will be included in the docs because it has a docstring
    """
    return unicode(self).encode('utf-8')

def __unicode__(self):
    # This will NOT be included in the docs
    return unicode(self.__class__.__name__)
```

This overrides Sphinx's `autodoc` setup.  So we can document all `__` methods which have a docstring, and avoid documenting all those which don't. I think that is probably what we want.

> Commas make lists unambiguous. While not required for a single integer, ['this',] != ['this']. Any iterable will be split into elements within the list if you don't mark them with commas. If you would like me to remove them, I will. Personally, I don't like to vary based on data types, so I choose to be explicit. If you have a pep I'd be happy to read and perhaps alter my opinion, but I've not read any thing authoritative against it. "Explicit is better than implicit." - https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0020/

No. List already are unambiguous. If you try and type in your example, you will see that ``['this'] == ['this',]`` returns True. Now of course if you use the type function on a something that is iterable ``list('this')`` something else entirely happens. The way that I understand it, the trailing comma is without any semantic meaning whatsoever (except for tuples), but are **allowed**, and the main reason for that, as I understand it, is to make diffs look cleaner if you have multiline definitions. If you have a counter examples here, I'd be thrilled to see it. As far as tuples go, it is purely out of necessity, that a single element tuple has to be defined with a trailing comma, because the parenthesis is also used to function calls. So while, as you say, maybe it would be better to not have to vary depending on type, I really prefer to look at as if it is consistent without trailing comma for almost all container types (list, set, dict) and the tuple is just an ugly exception that we have to live with, but which is also easy to remember because we all know we use parenthesis for functions.

As far as ``"Explicit is better than implicit."``, I'm very aware of the Zen of Python and I think it, and the general focus on readability, is one of the absolutely brilliant things about the language. I'm just not sure I think it applies here, or that it overrules ``"Although practicality beats purity."``and most especially ``"Readability counts."`` ;)

Thanks for the feedback - This does not entirely align with Scout2's purpose & vision, but some of it does. The tool was designed from a security auditor's point of view, therefore it provides a point-in-time snapshot of the account's security posture and its output is meant to be consumed by relatively AWS-savvy users (*i.e.* this was never the intent to have Scout2 generate CISO-ready -or readable- reports).

Scout2's primary goal is to provide security insights for AWS accounts or, as you put it, "help identify security faults with AWS environments". With that in mind, I believe the HTML report does its job. I believe it meets 3 of your 4 requirements:

* clear: AFAIK, people figure out quite easily how to browse the HTML report.
* human readable: in English, using a technology familiar to the vast majority of humans (HTML).
* concise: resource parameters that aren't security-relevant are stripped out, color highlighting is used to indicate the faulty setting for each finding.

While I appreciate when people learn new things after using the tool, Scout2 was not created with an educational goal so providing actionable, remediation recommendations is out of scope. Furthermore, AWS environments are complex and the actionable part can be quite tricky; some findings may be as easy as "delete the resource" or "enable this feature", while others would require complex changes in the reviewed architecture. Determining the right course of action requires investigation by someone with a good understanding of the system/resource under review.

That being said, exporting the output of the tool to CSV shouldn't be too hard assuming it's OK to change your suggested format
```
Finding #,Service,Subject,ARN,ZONE,Verdict,Finding Details,Remediation
```

into the following:

```
Finding #,Service,Subject,ARN,Level,Rationale
```

Changes are:

* Drop the zone (inconsistently stored / not always available / not necessary to identify the resource)
* Change the verdict to be the level (danger/warning) - Scout2 does not say anything about resources in a "good" state and this would go against the concise part anyway.
* Change the "finding details" to be the "rationale" why this rule exists (which is something that already exists for a number of rules).
* Remove the remediation part for the reasons listed above.

**Note** that the finding number would not be consistent across runs, just a counter that increments at each line (not sure it's worth adding).

Currently, spark-sklearn gives deprecation warnings when used with sklearn version .18 because several classes in grid_search and cross_validation were refactored into a new module called model_selection. The changes here make spark-sklearn compatible with the changes introduced in sklearn .18. 

The most critical changes reflected in the new version of sklearn is that:

sklearn.model_selection.GridSearchCV now has:

cv_results_ : dict of numpy (masked) ndarrays - A dict with keys as column headers and values as columns, that can be imported into a pandas DataFrame.
best_estimator_ : estimator - Estimator that was chosen by the search, i.e. estimator which gave highest score (or smallest loss if specified) on the left out data. Not available if refit=False.
best_score_ : float - Score of best_estimator on the left out data.
best_params_ : dict - Parameter setting that gave the best results on the hold out data.
best_index_ : int - The index (of the cv_results_ arrays) which corresponds to the best candidate parameter setting.
scorer_ : function - Scorer function used on the held out data to choose the best parameters for the model.
n_splits_ : int - The number of cross-validation splits (folds/iterations).
While spark-sklearn.GridSearchCV has:

grid_scores_ : list of named tuples
best_estimator_ : estimator - Estimator that was chosen by the search, i.e. estimator which gave highest score (or smallest loss if specified) on the left out data. Not available if refit=False.
best_score_ : float - Score of best_estimator on the left out data.
best_params_ : dict - Parameter setting that gave the best results on the hold out data.
scorer_ : function - Scorer function used on the held out data to choose the best parameters for the model.
The biggest is that sklearn added the more comprehensive cv_results_ which adds data that the formerly compatible grid_scores_ is lacking.

Note: This version of spark-sklearn is not compatible with sklearn <= .17.

Thanks Bo for the thorough analysis and for stepping up to do this.

I was thinking that we could try to make this filters support not necessarily tied with Glyphs.app and glyphsLib. I mean, would be nice if one could apply similar filters whether input is .glyphs or .ufo.

To that end, I think we could have ufo2ft instead of glyphsLib apply the actual filters on the Defcon glyphs before these are converted to fonttools objects.
If we do that, then both fontmake and other UFO based applications that use ufo2ft (e.g. Trufont) could take advantage of such on-export filters.

The job of glyphsLib would just be parsing the `Filter` custom parameters in the .glyphs source and writing that out to something that `ufo2ft` can consume, in the UFO lib. We could define a private  `com.github.googlei18n.ufo2ft.filters` UFO lib key, and use that to store these filter parameters.

The default filters would live in a sub-package, e.g. `ufo2ft.filters`, each in its own module. They could be based on the PointPen API (see `ufoLib/pointPen.py`), which is the standard way in the UFO world to manipulate outlines. They would work like "filter" pens, i.e. pens that take another pen that actually does the drawing, and before passing drawing commands to the inner pen, they do some transformations (the fonttools TransformPen is an example).

And after we implement the basic default filters, we could see how to make this extendable. Popular python applications like pytest or tox use the setuptools' `entry_points` feature to allow loading external plugins (e.g. http://doc.pytest.org/en/latest/writing_plugins.html#setuptools-entry-points). But we can look at that later, I think.

To answer Georg's suggestion about supporting existing third-party plugins written for Glyphs.app, I am not sure how that could be done from glyphsLib, which is supposed to run outside of and independently of Glyphs.app.

I've never written a Glyphs plugin, to be honest. However, looking at the documentation, it seems that one needs to import `from GlyphsApp.plugins import FilterWithoutDialog`, and this `GlyphsApp` module would not be accessible when running glyphsLib in a pure Python environment outside Glyphs.app.

https://github.com/schriftgestalt/GlyphsSDK/tree/master/Python%20Templates/Filter%20without%20Dialog

Let's think more about all this. Have a nice weekend!

Yeah, I hear you about it feeling like overkill, and executing SQL queries isn't nearly as Pythonic as dictionary-like access. But it's a single-table database whose interface is contained within the private methods of the module, so it's more like a tuple store with write locks. And `sqlite3` is built-in to Python and it's pretty light-weight (no need for a SQL server process or anything). The `sqlitedict` project has a nicer interface, for sure, but it incurs another dependency, as you say.

(Aside: my worries on #1221 about `pickle` not being compatible across Python versions were overblown; I reread the docs and it's seems to be mostly backward compatible, so `sqlitedict`'s use of `pickle` wouldn't be a problem) 

Alternatively, do you really need the ability to retrieve the original URL from a cached file? Using a simple hash of the filename would make the code a lot simpler.

`BaseClass.by_name('subclass_name')` must have already imported the code for `Subclass`.

the included `allennlp.run` command will not be aware of them. However, `allennlp.run` is simply
a wrapper around the `allennlp.commands.main` function,
which means you just need to create your own script
that imports all of your custom classes and then calls `allennlp.commands.main()`.

**Question for everyone:** does anyone value in-place linking? (i.e., when `link_df()` adds a `particle` column to an existing DataFrame, instead of making a new DataFrame that includes `particle`.) We have a lot of code devoted to it and it seems to perennially be causing bugs and surprises. The easiest fix (by far) to the problem identified by @mdgratale would be to keep `link_df()` but have it always make a copy.

In principle, in-place *should* be more memory-efficient, but this matters only when your data size is between ~50% and ~100% of available memory, your features come to you in a monolithic file format, and you need your linked tracks to all be in memory at the end. This sounds exactly like @mdgratale if he had 500,000 frames. Is that a likely possibility?

Another path to take here is to get a hold of a mkl complied version of numpy/scipy which (I think) will automatically parallelize the numpy/scipy operations under the hood.

We should probably also look into using dask, either at the low level image processing operations or the high-level frame-level.

The bottle neck in this operation changes depending both the image size and the number of features.  If you only have a few features, the bottle neck is almost certainly in the bandpass and dilation steps (if you have 10k particles the bottle neck moves to the localization/refinement).

If you only have a few features, it might be worth writing a version of batch that chops out sub-regions of your images to contain only the neighborhood around the particles.

I would also suggest making sure you actually zero out your black background, that will help with local-maximum finding.

I have one concern about the time-based throttling: if linking raises an exception, you won't know which frame caused the error.

Hi @scls19fr, thanks for raising this.  One way to look at this is that the issue is not so much with the libraries, but rather with the [JSON Schemas](http://json-schema.org/) that we're validating against.  Currently, `datapackage.json` files are validated using the JSON Schemas listed here: https://github.com/frictionlessdata/schemas.  The JSON Table Schema JSON Schema (say that three times fast) simply checks whether `format` is a "string" or not, but the validation of the `format` field should actually be dependent on the value of the `type` field.

There does seem to be a way to do this sort of conditional validation in JSON Schema (see [this answer on SO](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/9029524/json-schema-specify-field-is-required-based-on-value-of-another-field)).  Given that `format` and `type` validation of CSV is such a core part of our effort, this does seem to be something worth fixing.  I'd be curious to hear your thoughts :).

I haven't completely given up on it yet, but I'm starting to rethink the notion of an install wizard and wanted to share my thinking in case anyone else has some insight.

The OP just wanted an (easy) way to install without the storage subsystem components.  I wanted the same, and I thought an install wizard sounded like a great way to achieve that.  Making the installer _optional_ would mean no change to the existing install process for those who are already familiar with it and/or like the defaults, but the option to use the installer would mean even Deis novices would have access to certain configuration options (e.g. foregoing the storage subsystem) that they might struggle with otherwise.  Sounds ok in theory...

Fast forward a couple weeks and a new concern has crept onto my plate:

Every time I stand up a Deis cluster, I _also_ want to stand up a stack in that cluster for collecting and visualizing metrics.  I am using cadvisor + heapster + influxdb + grafana.  I want an easy way to launch these alongside Deis.  Maybe that could be a (future) option in the installer...

But wait... influxdb is a real pig in terms of CPU.  There's an observer effect at play.  When I am looking at CPU, I am affecting CPU.  :(  So, I not only want to install this stack alongside Deis, I'd want to tag one or more nodes as being the home for my metrics stack (which itself would _not_ be monitored) and I'd want to _modify_ all the Deis unit files so that Deis services are _not_ deployed to those metric-collecting nodes.  I'd want to keep my apps away from those metric-collecting nodes too.  So I'm into a situation where I'd need customized Deis unit files...

These are _my_ choices.  I wouldn't want to impose them on anyone else...

Well now we're way off the beaten path in terms of what an install wizard could reasonably be expected to address, right?  It seems the answer (for me, personally) is to create some _custom_ tooling that will install and launch not only Deis, but also my customizations to it, _and_ all the other stuff I need to run alongside it... and I'm thinking I can't be the only one who has this issue-- that they have a whole ton of other stuff that they need to run in their Deis cluster and they maybe even need to modify until files (or images) to make that work.

So, to bring this all full circle, maybe the answer is _not_ a wizard.  Maybe what is needed is a _general_ framework for Fleet (implemented as a Go library) that allows tools built upon it to orchestrate the installation and launch of services.  `deisctl` could be refactored to be built upon such a framework (but the guts of that framework could easily be harvested from the _existing_ `deisctl` implementation).

This may not prove as useful to a novice operator as an installer, but it would create a lot of room for open source _and_ proprietary tools to emerge that can launch specific (possibly customized) Deis configurations.  Hell, it could even be used to launch non-Deis stuff into a Fleet cluster.

Is this a better option than a wizard?  Or am I totally nuts?

Funny you should bring this up, because it's been on my mind for the last few days, too. This is partly because I have been looking at the k8s build process, and partly because I have been working on various tests that I still can't get to reliably run locally (even though they're fine on Jenkins).

I feel like a containerized build/test environment would be a big boon. It would (I really, really hope) greatly simplify the requirements for developing on and testing Deis, make the testing environment more consistent with deployment environments (viz. not testing on Mac and then running on Linux). And I think it would give us the ability to keep development environments synchronized to a greater degree.

A few ideas about this:

- I have been wondering if it would make the most sense to have two Docker imaages:
    *  A base image (call it `deis-dev-environment`) that contained things like the Go, Python, PostgreSQL, etc. -- the toolchain, essentially.
    * A build image, which copied the Git checkout into a container, and then did all the actual building and testing.
- For the expediency of development, any suitable system would have to have the following:
    * The ability to re-build components individually. (If you're hacking on the router, it's a waste of time to rebuild the entire system every time). We do this now with `make -C component deploy`.
    * The ability to turn of the testing, since sometimes in the dev cycle we want to build but not run the entire test suite.
- Now that we're switching to Docker Machine, we might be able to more tightly couple the dev registry with the dev environment, which is desirable in this case.

The problem here is actually pretty simple to explain.

After the first run, `conda` "downloads" `foobar-1.0.0-0.tar.bz2` into its cache from the `conda-bld` channel. During the second run, _it's already in the cache_, so it doesn't think it needs to download it again.

The "problem" from conda-build's perspective is that conda is doing a better job of keeping track of the packages in its cache, and what channels it came from. This is absolutely the right thing for us to do for typical conda usage, but clearly it is problematic for conda-build. It doesn't know that the version of the tarball it has in its cache has expired, and I'm not sure it has any way of knowing this.

One simple solution would be to remove the tarball from `pkgs` before building the test environment.

`pandas` itself does not depend on `mkl`.  What matters is whether or not the `nomkl` meta-package is installed.  If `nomkl` is installed, then the non-MKL versions of `numpy`, `scipy`, etc. are preferred over the MKL linked versions (regardless of what package you install).

I believe I've found the root cause of the issue, and it is not simply trying to write to the home dir. That is actually a pretty reasonable expectation for most programs.

Instead, the code of [mkdir_p_sudo_safe](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/c72c237a354c3fd9629d800311ba6a2b8a95d1d1/conda/gateways/disk/__init__.py#L69) makes the bad assumption that any use of `sudo` with Anaconda is a user mistake, and that it should just write files as the original user instead (from the `SUDO_UID` env var). 

In my case using sudo is correct and intended. I'm automating the installation of JupyterHub using Ansible, and I *want* to drop from root to an unprivileged user to create a virtual environment that can be read by that user. I do not want any files to be created as root, and the home directory is correctly set by Ansible.

Studying your output more, I think there were several things that happened.  First, as of conda 4.1 and later, channel order matters.  Also, `conda config --add channels conda-canary` actually has different behavior in 4.1 and 4.2 (changelog [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/releases/tag/4.2.0).  In 4.1, it is equivalent to `conda config --prepend channels conda-canary`.  In 4.2, it is equivalent to `conda config --append channels conda-canary`.  The behavior change is deliberate.  It's a safer default behavior for more inexperienced users.

I'm guessing your condarc has something like

```
channels:
  - defaults
  - conda-canary
```

which is why `conda update --all` wanted to downgrade conda.

The "going haywire" part with trash removal is what I'm more concerned about and don't have a good explanation for yet.

So two proposals seem to be:

(a) A package file in a channel is determined by (in order of specificity) the (major, minor, patch) (referring to upstream information) and (build string, build number, file hash) (referring to the conda packaging process). Any update in the hash is *automatically* updated (i.e., newer hash override older hash automatically). How to compare hashes is unresolved. One proposal is to keep the set of previous hashes in the package metadata, so if you have two iterations, one of the file's hashes will be in the other packages metadata. 

(b) A package file in a channel is determined by (in order of specificity) the (major, minor, patch) (referring to upstream information) and (build string, build number) (referring to the conda packaging process). The build number is an integer so can be naturally compared. Newer build metadata automatically overrides older build metadata.

So basically, hotfixing a build essentially moves the pinning process down one level. Arguably, if you're really trying to guarantee a specific package file, you should be pinning to a hash because that can be checked.

This isn't a regression.  Curator 4.0 only supports ES versions 2.x and up. See the [Versioning](https://github.com/elastic/curator#versioning) of the README.  It seems you're using the API and creating your own client connection, as the `get_client` method supplied by Curator would have detected this and disallowed it.

The longer reason is that there is a ton of new functionality that is simply not supported by older versions of Elasticsearch.  Much of this new functionality _depends_ on using the cluster metadata calls, which are disallowed by AWS ES.  

Curator 3.x persists for those who are compelled, for whatever reason, to stay in that space.  Trying to straddle major versions, and document & instruct users which actions and filters do or do not work based on Elasticsearch versions is a support nightmare.  As a result, a decision was made to cut off support for older versions of ES with the newer version of Curator.

If you're using AWS ES, you'll need to use the Curator 3.x branch.  You can install this via pip with `pip install -U elasticsearch-curator==3.5.1`

The more I think about this the wider the pendulum swings in both directions.

I really like the simplicity of the Class/Object, but its complexity grows quickly if you do not specify default values.  I do not want any default values.  This is one of the reasons for the endless `if` statements in `index_selection` and the associated methods.  I want fairly strident controls forcing the user to specify things like `days`, and `timestring`.  If I'm already going to have that, as complex as it is, it makes some sense to keep the formatting the way it is.  I also want to have the process mimic the way the CLI is filtering (callbacks are adding the filters one at a time), which is slightly different from how the class handles it (and I suppose you could have the class instance be part of the obj dictionary, but that's a future discussion).

On the other hand, it makes some sense to add the other method as an alternative way to add items.  It's clean, it's simple, and many users may wind up preferring it.

So, rather than choose one or the other, **why not both?**  There's no reason not to have both!  Use the API call that you like.

Because it so closely mimics the way the command-line works, I'm including the `build_filter` method as shown, including the examples.  

In addition, I would absolutely *love* for you, @SegFaultAX, to add this class as an alternative method to add index filters, complete with defaults as you have them.  Please add the unit tests in the appropriate files.  I definitely want to keep the 99% code coverage the project currently has.  Please also thoroughly document the class such that sphinx will pick it up.  We can go over any and all changes before merging.

In this way I hope we can pave a way for the future, while over-filling the needs of the present.

The `creation_date` is index metadata that did not get added until Elasticsearch 1.4.  That's why that message is in Curator.  Your index selection criteria is probably using an `age` filter with `creation_date`, and this is the result if the index has no `creation_date` metadata (and that metadata is in the cluster state, not the index itself).  Curator tries to do the safe thing and not act on an index in that case.

@martindurant This is a robustness and resiliency scenario. You can only ever append at the end of a file, however if we have retry logic in our append requests, we could end up appending duplicate data, since the server doesn't care if you append the same data to the end of the file twice. By explicitly telling the server where you think the end of the file is, you can prevent this. The server will look at the offset you supplied and, if it is the end of the file as you expect, then everything works as normal. If it is not (meaning that a previous append succeeded on the server, even though the front end may have reported a failure), then you can mark the current chunk as completed and move on.

The goal isn't to change the resume/restart functionality, but to do our best in the client to ensure that, when we finish the upload, we are reasonably confident that we only uploaded the exact data we were given, resulting in a file that is identical to the one supplied.

This appears to be a number of things:
- The version of setuptools is too old to support "wheel" distribution information
- pre-commit>=0.9.0 [no longer supports python2.6](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/844c839067b530e7598e6c6afd59b565b091e19c/CHANGELOG.md#090)
- pre-commit's dependency (at 0.8.1 (but [no longer](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/3874636f8f71aed8c2ab472db828475a549d1479/CHANGELOG.md#0134)!)) jsonschema [no longer supports python2.6](https://github.com/Julian/jsonschema/blob/master/CHANGELOG.rst#v260)

That said, I was able to get this to hobble along by doing the following things (note: upgrading packages managed by the system package manager aren't usually a good idea -- so don't necessarily do what I do!)

```dockerfile
FROM centos:6.9

RUN yum install -y git
WORKDIR /repo
RUN git init /repo
RUN curl https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py | python && \
    # Need to do this in a separate command before installing pre-commit as
    # pre-commit has non-wheeled dependencies that require setuptools to
    # build from source.
    # Also need to install a sufficiently new setuptools from pip instead of
    # using the `python-setuptools` package through yum (which is at version
    # 0.6rc11: released in 2009: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/setuptools/0.6c11)
    pip install setuptools && \
    # Note, jsonschema is a dependency of pre-commit which has also dropped
    # python2.6 support, so we need to limit to a version that will install
    # there as well.
    pip install 'jsonschema<=2.5.999' 'pre-commit<=0.8.999'
CMD ["pre-commit", "install"]
```

Output:

```console
$ docker run -ti test
pre-commit installed at /repo/.git/hooks/pre-commit
```

So in several different ways, python2.6 (and centos6) are just too old to work.

```yaml
# configure shared hooks that you want to run on all repositories here
-   repo: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks.git
    sha: v0.6.1

    -   id: trailing-whitespace

    -   id: flake8

    -   id: flake8
        name: 'flake8 (for repo2)'
        # Only run on repo2 files
        files: '^repo2/.*\.py'
        # maybe repo2 wants specific argument configuration

    # maybe repo3 is python3 only

```

Note that the same `repo` can be configured multiple times and within the repo the same hook id can also be repeated

ok!  Let's discuss the ergonomics of such a feature and the possible approaches for that -- I'll give you code hints for each of them :)

1. The first option is to pass an environment variable that pre-commit picks up on at commit time.
_this is the one I'd prefer if it fits your usecase_

This would follow the precedent set by [`SKIP`](https://pre-commit.com/#temporarily-disabling-hooks).

You'd get output something like this:

```console
$ PRE_COMMIT_VERBOSE=1 git commit -m 'test'
[trailing-whitespace] Trim Trailing Whitespace...........................Passed
hookid: trailing-whitespace

output if it had some

[master (root-commit) ba90d2e] test
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 .pre-commit-config.yaml


$ git commit -m "test"
Trim Trailing Whitespace...........................Passed
[master (root-commit) ba90d2e] test
 1 file changed, 5 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 .pre-commit-config.yaml
```

This would probably be done by changing the default for `--verbose` based on `os.environ.get('PRE_COMMIT_VERBOSE')` [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f042e302d8d871044ed7b21f44d8f060cefb41cc/pre_commit/main.py#L60)

You'd write a test for this behaviour [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f042e302d8d871044ed7b21f44d8f060cefb41cc/tests/commands/install_uninstall_test.py#L139-L146) (I've highlighted a similar test that you could adjust)

This to me feels like the nicest behaviour, `verbose` should really be used sparingly as the more warning noise there is the more likely people are going to ignore the output (or worse, turn it off!).

2. The second option would be to add a top-level configuration value for the `.pre-commit-config.yaml`

This would look something like:

```yaml
verbose: true
hooks:
    # ...
```

To add to the configuration schema, you'd add a property [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f042e302d8d871044ed7b21f44d8f060cefb41cc/pre_commit/clientlib.py#L151) (I've highlighted a similar property).

You would then update the behaviour of `run` inside the `run` command, probably [hereish](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f042e302d8d871044ed7b21f44d8f060cefb41cc/pre_commit/commands/run.py#L226) (not exactly sure on the best place to put that tbh, right now it is being accessed via `args` (argparse return value) which isn't terribly typesafe or easy to reason about -- could mutate `args` but that also feels wrong -- maybe make a new `namedtuple` to represent the values that `run` needs and then adjust that based on the configuration value? not sure.

You'd probably add a test [here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f2b74e33870439934ce7f59406b761e2b41d8a27/tests/commands/run_test.py#L283-L296) (I've highlighted a similar test which sets `verbose` at a specific hook level).

This has the one downside that if one of your consumers doesn't want the verbose behaviour they don't really have a way to turn it off.

3. The last option, and imo the one that has the last visibility would be to write the option to the hook template during the install stage.  Something like:

```console
$ pre-commit install --use-verbose
$ git commit -m '...'
(verbose output
```

Something about this irks me but I can't quite put my finger on it, perhaps it's that I want the hook template to be as logic free as possible since it's really difficult to test / debug / reason about (and the end user visibility here is really poor).

That said, there is [precedent via `pre-commit install --skip-on-missing-config`](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f2b74e33870439934ce7f59406b761e2b41d8a27/pre_commit/commands/install_uninstall.py#L78).  This adds to the [hook template](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f2b74e33870439934ce7f59406b761e2b41d8a27/pre_commit/resources/hook-tmpl#L17) and then affects only the invocation of `git commit` afterwards.

You'd probably test this in the same place as option 1, though you'd probably follow [this test](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/f042e302d8d871044ed7b21f44d8f060cefb41cc/tests/commands/install_uninstall_test.py#L693-L710) 

Hope this is helpful, let me know what you think!
Thanks!

Failing when no credentials are configured has been the behavior since the very first version of this hook. An obvious solution would be of course to simple remove the following lines:
https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/master/pre_commit_hooks/detect_aws_credentials.py#L114-L120

I agree that removing those lines would allow silent failures in cases where a user with credentials in some obscure location installs the hook and expects it to work without having tested it.

My proposal to solve this problem is to simply introduce an additional argument for the script, providing the ability to simply disable failures when no credentials are found, but keeping the current behavior the default.

> But isn't the semantic of null/`None`, in the context of a `IntType` field, just "there is no value here"? The only time we have to distinguish "there is no value here" and "there is no value here and ignore this when using me as a patch" is when dealing with a "partial" model.

Correct.

I totally get your point that it would be kind of nice to be able to isolate the "unset" situation so that you don't accidentally have a mix of `None` and `Undefined` fields when you only intended to use explicit `None`s.

But the partial models *must* support validation and exporting *as is*. That is an absolute requirement; otherwise the whole thing will be half-baked. In other words, we have to be able to deal with the input

```python
{'a': 1, 'b': None}
```

and validate it and export it without merging it with a full model and having it turn into

```python
{'a': 1, 'b': None, 'c': None}
```

The idea of creating a full-blown parallel class to represent a model's partial data is likely a non-starter because all its members would have to be kept in sync with the master copies on the main class. Absolutely everything would have to implement `__getattribute__()` and intercept all operations and make sure they are performed in the parallel universe as well. Instance and subclass checks and equality comparisons would all break unless re-implemented in the metaclass.

However, I suppose in theory we *could* have a `PartialModel` class that simply delegates everything to the main model. But how, then, is this any better than simply using a regular instance of the main model and tagging it as `m.patch=True`? What does the separate class bring to the table?

***

The general idea does warrant examination, though.

That is, should we implement a `Model.patch` property that would indicate the kind of each instance? For *patch instances* the unset/`None` distinction would exist, but for *full instances* it would not. This is certainly worth considering.

```python
>>> m = M({'a': 1, 'b': None})
>>> del m.a
>>> m.a # None
>>> m.b # None
>>> m.c # None
```

```python
>>> m = M({'a': 1, 'b': None}, patch=True)
>>> del m.a
>>> m.a
MissingValueError
>>> m.b # None
>>> m.c
MissingValueError
```

Essentially, `patch` would subsume `init_values` in the constructor, but instead of only affecting instance creation, it would have a persistent effect.

The property would not be static. You could freely switch from one kind to the other. Going from `patch=True` to `patch=False` would imply the conversion of every `Undefined` to `None`.

To me, this seems pretty reasonable with no obvious drawbacks. It might even end up delivering some benefits beyond simply guarding against accidental mixing of different concepts of "no value".

What do you think?

Hey everyone, great job on the pull requests so far!  Just a quick update on what we expect from `get_mutable_handler()` and `get_immutable_handler()`:

The driver must be written such that once a user writes data, *any other user* can read it given its `data_id` (from `get_mutable_handler()`) or its `key` (from `get_immutable_handler()`).  The other users will *not* have the writer's API tokens, access credentials, or system-specific public keys.  In all three of these systems, this basically means you have to use the [indexing API](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/blob/rc-0.14.2/blockstack_client/backend/drivers/common.py) to build up an index that resolves `data_id` and `key` values to the URIs that these systems generate.

The indexing system works by creating a top-level "manifest" URI that the user can attach to their profile.    That is, given a blockchain ID (passed as `fqu` in the drivers' keyword arguments), the indexing system can look up a user's index manifest URI.  Using this index manifest URI, the indexing system will fetch and store segments of the index to read, write, and delete `(data_id, URI)` mappings.  The top-level methods of interest are:

* `driver_config()`: sets up callbacks to be used by the indexer code for loading and storing both data and pages of the index.  Returns a configuration structure to be passed into the below methods.


* `get_indexed_data()`: loads data from the storage by translating an app-given name into a service-specific URI.

* `put_indexed_data()`: stores data with a given name into the storage system, and inserts an entry for it in an index alongside the data.

* `delete_indexed_data()`: removes data with a given name from the storage system, and updates the co-located index to remove its name-to-URI link.

* `index_setup()`: instantiates an index (callable from the driver's `storage_init()` method).

You can see examples in the `test.py` and `dropbox.py` drivers on how to use this subsystem.

I have written a driver test harness to help confirm that your driver uses them correctly, located [here](https://github.com/blockstack/blockstack-core/blob/rc-0.14.2/integration_tests/bin/blockstack-storage-driver-test).  You have to test the drivers using two hosts:  a "reader" host and a "writer" host.

To test the "writer", I'd do something like the following if I were testing the Dropbox driver:

```
    $ ssh jude@writer
    writer:$ cd ~/blockstack_core/integration_tests/bin
    writer:$ cat > /tmp/writer-config.ini <<EOF
    [dropbox]
    token = $MY_DROPBOX_TOKEN
    EOF
    writer:$ # instantiate some test data
    writer:$ cat ../datasets/test-datasets-dropbox.json.in | sed -r 's~@MANIFEST_URI@~~g' > /tmp/test-datasets.json
    writer:$ ./blockstack-storage-driver-test --debug --config /tmp/writer-config.ini --index --datasets /tmp/test-datasets.json blockstack_client.backend.drivers.dropbox put
    # ...
    
        index manifest URI: https://www.dropbox.com/s/ie8ab7qc8e5i0o3/index.manifest?dl=1

    writer:$ logout
```

Using the given URI above, I can now test the dropbox reader as follows:

```
    $ ssh jude@reader
    reader:$ cd blockstack-core/integration_tests/bin
    reader:$ cat ../datasets/test-datasets-dropbox.json.in | sed -r 's~@MANIFEST_URI@~https://www.dropbox.com/s/ie8ab7qc8e5i0o3/index.manifest?dl=1~g' > /tmp/test-datasets.json
    reader:$ ./blockstack-storage-driver-test --debug --index --datasets /tmp/test-datasets.json blockstack_client.backend.drivers.dropbox get
```

No Dropbox API token should be necessary for the last command to succeed.  This is because the write paths in the Dropbox driver instantiated an index in my Dropbox account, and printed out the URI to the index manifest after writing some data.

If the user was connecting Dropbox to their account, what would normally happen is that this URI would be automatically added to the user's profile in a canonical location.  This way, readers can proceed to access the writer's Dropbox-hosted data, despite the fact that Dropbox does not let readers simply construct URIs to data they did not write (like how they might in S3).

What we do to simulate this in the test harness is we use a "template" list of datasets to replicate and manually pass the manifest URI generated by the writer to the reader by means of including it as a driver hint.  By doing so, the reader will succeed at resolving the data saved by the writer to the appropriate URI in Dropbox, which can then be fetched via HTTP.

Anyway, please experiment with this test harness with Dropbox, and please confirm that your drivers behave under similar circumstances.  Thanks!

Hi,

There is no function to do that for now, but it should not be too difficult to implement. The most triky part is to check the dict is valid (correct entries...)

`BibDatabase` is fragile to modification of `entries`. For example:

```python
import bibtexparser

db = bibtexparser.bibdatabase.BibDatabase()
db.entries = [{'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Foo'}, {'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Hehe'}]
db.entries
>>> [{'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Foo'}, {'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Hehe'}]
db.get_entry_dict()
>>> {'foo': {'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Hehe'}}
```

Moreover, the method [`BibDatabase.get_entry_dict`](https://github.com/sciunto-org/python-bibtexparser/blob/d86d7873ac5e1550b86b0985f722cfeecee22fc2/bibtexparser/bibdatabase.py#L52) does not work if the attribute `entries` is modified:

```python
db.entries = [{'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Foo'}, {'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Hehe'}]
db.get_entry_dict()
>>> {'foo': {'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Hehe'}}
db.entries = None
db.get_entry_dict()
>>> {'foo': {'ID': 'foo', 'title': 'Hehe'}}
```

because only `_entries_dict` is memoized. The `list` `entries` needs to be memoized too, e.g., as `_entries`, and the check replaced with whether `_entries` matches `entries`:

```python
# initialize
self.entries = list()
self._entries = None
self._entries_dict = None

# and then in `self.get_entry_dict()`
if self._entries == self.entries:
    return self._entries_dict
self._entries = list(self.entries)
for entry in self.entries:
    self._entries_dict[entry['ID']] = entry
return self._entries_dict
```
If memory and time are not of concern (i.e., most people probably don't work with millions of bib entries -- it depends on the user base), then the best solution is to simply return `dict(self.entries)`, without any memoization.

I do understand that the current implementation is supposed to be simple and small (which is good) and one-pass, but:

1. some small modifications, like memoizing `entries`, don't sacrifice simplicity. They may matter for performance, but it seems that optimization is not the primary goal here.

2. it would be good to remark about these limitations in the docstrings or user documentation.

Moreover, it would be interesting to merge multiple bibtex files. However, as remarked [above](https://github.com/sciunto-org/python-bibtexparser/issues/115#issuecomment-198324318), this introduces the issue of duplicate BibTeX *keys*. Note that this issue can also arise when loading entries from a single BibTeX file, because the file is not a `dict` (unless its creation guarantees uniqueness -- for example editing by hand does not). Therefore, this is a valid issue to resolve (I am interested on remarks and preferences, because I am working on some functions for that purpose).

Replicated entries shouldn't be a problem. Keeping a unique representative for each set of replicas suffices. However, this is about exact entry replicas. Entries with identical keys but differences in other BibTeX fields should be considered as different entries. In particular, they should be identified as similar, and dumping them lists them grouped (due to lexicographic sorting of keys), so that the user can then easily proceed with merging (this is what I am working on).

Even better, a report of how much entries differ can be produced by using the [Levenshtein distance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Levenshtein_distance) on the values of the differing fields, e.g., using the [`distance`](https://github.com/doukremt/distance) package.

Ok, I found the issue. Readthedocs has some magic algorithm to determine what the latest "stable" release is. It seems that our `2.0` branch is confusing that algorithm (2.0 > 0.5.3): readthedocs thinks our 2.0 branch is the latest "stable" release.

I renamed our 2.0 branch to `2.0a1` and now the algorithm does the correct thing: `stable` is the latest tagged release (0.5.3 now) and `latest` is the `master` branch.

Okay, here's some useful NetLogo commands that I am not sure are already in Mesa. Mesa obviously relies on standard Python for concepts like agentsets (for example, selecting agents that satisfy some condition), mathematical operators and networks (perhaps igraph or NetworkX), so I didn't really look at those. In this list, I have used the NetLogo primitive name - full descriptions are available at the [dictionary](https://ccl.northwestern.edu/netlogo/docs/dictionary.html).

Agent movement:
- uphill/downhill: move in the direction of an increasing/decreasing value of a characteristic owned by the space
- face: change heading so that a forward movement will go toward a specified agent
- turtles-on: create a set of all the agents at a particular grid reference
- patch-ahead: where an agent would move in the next time step (eg to check for obstacles)
Agent selection:
- max-n-of, min-n-of: select the n agents with the largest/smallest values of a specified attribute
- weighted-n-of: select randomly in proportion to the value of a specified attribute (part of the rnd extension)

There's also 'self' and 'myself'. These are terrible names, but the concept is essential to agent-centric thinking. One agent needs to be able to ask another agent to do something (such as change an attribute value) and there needs to be an easy way to distinguish between the agent doing the asking and the agent being asked.

We need to install our dependencies before we can install ourselves and be able to produce output for `--help`.

`letsencrypt certonly` will issue certificates only. `letsencrypt certonly -a webroot --webroot-path /var/www/html`, `letsencrypt certonly -a standalone`, and `letsencrypt certonly -a manual` will not touch your Apache configs.

There are plenty of people that find it very hard to configure their webservers properly. While specifying a path to certificate might not be that hard (although you would be surprised), properly configuring cipher suites is a very complicated process.

It's going to be tricky to fix this without breaking the design constraints of letsencrypt-auto, which are:
* it's a wrapper script that's a temporary stopgap before native packages are available on your OS
* it should accept the exact same flags as the the true `letsencrypt` and wrap it transparently

For instance, we could have `letsencrypt-auto --help` baked into the script.  But what if the user types `letsencrypt-auto --help certonly`?  They still won't expect installation as a side effect, but it will be necessary.

Perhaps we can adjust the docs to not suggest `letsencrypt-auto --help` as a first command or something, and find some other first-run command that is free of consequences but connotes installation.

To elaborate on @pde's comments (which I moved to the first post in this issue), I tried to characterize some actions we could take to reduce or dependencies. While I believe reducing our dependencies is a worthwhile goal, it is worth noting that it will slow down development on other features of the client.

### Remove dependency on git
This is going away in `0.2.0` with the changes to `letsencrypt-auto`.

### Remove psutil, cryptography, and PyOpenSSL
This would be a major change. We currently use the three packages for the following:

##### psutil
* Used to tell the user what process is listening on the port we want

##### cryptography
* SHA2
* HMAC
* RSA 
    - PSS and PKCS1v15 padding
    - Help with RSA to/from JSON
* Serializing/parsing keys and certs
* Loading/saving/converting keys

##### PyOpenSSL
* Generating RSA keys
* Serializing/Parsing certs and CSRs
    - Getting data like notBefore/notAfter values from certs
* TLS handshake
* Creating certs/CSRs
* Loading/saving/converting certs and CSRs

Removing `psutil` may be a minor usability loss and removing `cryptography` and `PyOpenSSL` would be a lot of work. We could likely replace all/most of this functionality with calls to the `openssl` command line. I don't think that's as clean from a code quality perspective, but it would allow us to drastically reduce our dependencies. Removing these three dependencies removes the following additional packages:

##### OS packages:
* gcc 
* python-dev
* libffi-dev
* libssl-dev

##### Python packages
* idna
* enum34
* ipaddress
* cffi
* pycparser

### Consolidate werkzeug, pytz, parsedatetime, and pyrfc3339 into one package

There packages are all for parsing/manipulating dates and may are only used in one or two places in the code. Do we really need four packages for this or can we consolidate them? Their current uses are:

##### werkzeug (used exactly once in the code)
* parse value of Retry-After header

##### pytz
* specifying UTC timezone to other libraries

##### parsedatetime
* determining the difference between two date/times

##### pyrfc3339
* you guessed it: parse/generate RFC3339 timestamp

### Consolidate/remove configargparse/configobj
These packages are both used for processing config files. They do slightly different things, but we could remove `configargparse` and only use `configobj`. It'd be slightly hacky and only allows us to drop one package, but we have code that does a lot of what `configargparse` does using `configobj` in `renewer.py`.

With this said, both projects are largely unmaintained and perhaps we should consider other alternatives.

### Install pyasn1 and ndg-httpsclient only when needed
These packages are used to fix the `InsecurePlatformWarning` that occurs while using `urllib3` with older versions of Python. We currently always install these packages and doing so is not necessary.

'attribute' means to set attribute name of the same schema, not nested object.

 [As the document writes:](http://marshmallow.readthedocs.org/en/latest/quickstart.html#specifying-attribute-names)
> Specifying Attribute Names
> By default, Schemas will marshal the object attributes that are identical to the schema’s field names. However, you may want to have different field and attribute names. In this case, you can explicitly specify which attribute names to use.
> 

```
> class UserSchema(Schema):
>     name = fields.String()
>     email_addr = fields.String(attribute="email")
>     date_created = fields.DateTime(attribute="created_at")
> 
> user = User('Keith', email='keith@stones.com')
> ser = UserSchema()
> result, errors = ser.dump(user)
> pprint(result)
> # {'name': 'Keith',
> #  'email_addr': 'keith@stones.com',
> #  'date_created': '2014-08-17T14:58:57.600623+00:00'}
```

> **dump_only** - If True skip this field during deserialization, otherwise its value will be present in the deserialized object. In the context of an HTTP API, this effectively marks the field as “read-only”.

https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/3.0/api_reference.html#marshmallow.fields.Field

"skip" implies that the field is allowed, but it will be excluded. This is consistent with the `unknown=INCLUDE` behavior, but not `unknown=ERROR`. I think to make this consistent we would either need to soften the "unknown" validation for `dump_only`, or harden the definition of `dump_only` to include validation.

> with `unknown=RAISE`: `dump_only` ... wouldn't it be better to ignore the dump-only fields during loading and not raise error on them (as we're explicitly saying we are not interested about these fields during loading)?

I don't think so. The goal of `unknown=RAISE` is to warn developers that they are passing in data that is going to be lost. I think we should harden the definition of `dump_only` so that it doesn't violate the expectations set by the `unknown` option.

If `dump_only` meant "don't allow loading", then `unknown=ERROR | INCLUDE` would error and only explicitly opting into `unknown=EXCLUDE` would allow the data to be discarded.

> > `validate_<field name>(self, value)`
> 
> I've never really liked this API in Django forms and WTForms. It is redundant API and a bit too "magical". I think we can defer implementing this.

If you're going to make everything else methods, might as well go all the way.  Currently, you can put field-specific validation as a lambda in the validators list, or make an external function.  Lambdas are convenient *if* the validation is not complex and can fit on one line.  Functions are convenient only if they're likely to be reused, which is not the case most times I want a `validate_<field name>` method.  The convenience of having field-specific validation outweighs the perceived magic.

Writing custom field classes would work, of course... but if I have to write a custom Field subclass for every single data type, I'll just go ahead and roll my own type conversion/validation module and save myself the import.  Indeed, that's what I ended up doing for the project where I was evaluating marshmallow.

It's a bit of a shame, because marshmallow has somef features that make it play nice with ElementTree (by accident I suppose).  This is a strength that could be capitalized upon.

Taking a step back - the meaning of an empty string is defined _globally_ by the protocol and/or parsing library.... "emtpy string is NULL" is a common choice.  To map that protocol into Python data types, you want to write that equivalence _once_, globally... not write the same boilerplate ten times, once per validator.  That's most inelegant.

Setting my use case aside, you've got users saying "hey wait a minute, HTML forms also use 'empty string is NULL' for lots of different data types".   To me that sounds like a fairly key use case for marshmallow, and not handling it in the library seems like a bit of an oversight.

Just a friendly $0.02, because I see a lot to like in marshmallow.  I probably would have used it for my project if this feature existed.

Okay, we have docs that build the environment, including gromacs 5.1, using conda: https://readthedocs.org/projects/mdsynthesis/builds/3881150/

But, when building the class docs for the building blocks, we get errors because the config we ship with is for gromacs 4.x, and so things like ``grompp`` can't be found. Quick and dirty solution is to replace current [gromacswrapper.cfg](https://github.com/Becksteinlab/GromacsWrapper/blob/develop/gromacs/templates/gromacswrapper.cfg) with one that works with 5.x names. Longer-term solution is #49.

We cannot install a gromacs 4.x version on readthedocs with conda because there are are no 4.x packages available on the anaconda cloud. If we want this, we'd have to build our own first.

Creating a repository always comes with responsibility. In some ways, shipping MDPs with GW is already a bit of a burden but given it's hackish nature (and the convenience for me...) made it ok. Building good repositories is not that easy – we created [Lipidbook](http://lipidbook.bioch.ox.ac.uk/) and [Ligandbook](http://ligandbook.org/) and that was hard work to get right. [CHARMM-GUI](http://www.charmm-gui.org/) ships Gromacs input files and I know that people are using them directly.

I use the stuff in `gromacs.setup` but I honestly don't know who else and that's the only part where the MDP files are used in GW.

And truth be told – if my students told me they were doing simulations with MDP files they found on the internet I would probably tell them to `rm -rf`, read the appropriate papers, and then create MDP files with the appropriate settings. (Actually, more likely I'd tell them to read the papers and to use one of the MDP files that we keep internally but these are still the settings that we as a lab decided to use and other people might disagree.) 

Basically, I don't want to shoulder all the responsibility for "correct" input files. (And yes, `gromacs.setup` does go in this direction but at least the MDP files have a big, fat disclaimer...).

Bottomline: If you can come up with a workable solution that won't allow people to blame bad simulations on us then I am happy to listen to ideas. I wouldn't make it part of GW per se, though – if anything, rather make the package slimmer (along the discussion #82 and name space packages). Perhaps the idea of an input generator would be a good compromise: still requires user input but you can give some a bit of a helping hand and catch some common mistakes in advance (e.g., the plethora of cutoffs for non-bonded interactions...).

I would 'use as is'. 

Whenever you submit a PR is fine. It's open source, you contribute when you can. (And you will be listed as an author of GW).

It looks like that's actually pretty tricky. There seems to be an assumption (in pip and the like) that setup.py only needs stuff lower in the directory structure. For example, if I do `pip install /path/to/z3/scripts/packages/pip`, pip creates the build directory from that directory, so the rest of Z3 is nowhere to be found.

One possible solution would be to keep the setup scripts in another repo, and have them clone the main repo if needed. From personal experience, this is a bit of a pain in terms of maintenance, usability, and support.

I believe there is some miss-understanding: del_context does in fact deallocate the context. 
Any dangling references are invalid. This is innocent for dangling AST objects where 
the memory gets reclaimed, but not too good for dangling solver and ast_vector 
objects whose memory is not reclaimed 
(given the current architecture, which your question raises a good point about).

The Z3_resest_memory/finalize_memory reclaim memory allocated to symbols and big-num rationals. This is across contexts and helps so that you can unload Z3 dlls without leaving allocated memory around.

@msullivan Note the python build system (i.e. ``python scripts/mk_make.py``) doesn't work properly if you use reuse an existing build directory or change parts of the build (e.g. change build flags or change a module parameter). It also can sometimes fail to rebuild correctly if updating the code to a newer version (e.g. ``git pull``).

The CMake build system doesn't suffer from these issues (which is why I created it) but you will need to teach it how to handle the ARM architectures. Look for uses of ``TARGET_ARCHITECTURE`` in the CMake files to see where it's currently being used and modify as necessary. You will also need to modify https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/blob/master/contrib/cmake/cmake/target_arch_detect.cpp

Merging! Thanks again!

> it was nice to contribute a little to it!

I'd be glad to see you again! :) We could definitely use some help to finally get 0.2 finished (https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/milestone/2).

> We could discuss adding some more keyword arguments to get_output_for(), though. Right now deterministic=False uses the minibatch-dependent statistics and updates the running means, and deterministic=True uses the running means. Actually, the former is not really indeterministic, it just happens to match with how we usually obtain the training and test predictions. We could have some extra keyword arguments like batch_norm_update_averages and batch_norm_use_averages to override the default behaviour.

I think the most pragmatic approach here is to overload `deterministic` here, even if it's not 100% correct. I've always interpreted it as "disable any sources of stochasticity in the network", and I guess batchnorm qualifies as a source of stochasticity when you randomly sample the minibatch that a given example happens to be part of.

The main downside to this is that it makes it harder to selectively disable only dropout or only batchnorm. But I'm not sure if that's a use case we should consider. In that case I guess you should just rebuild the network with/without the specified layers.

> In #429, we thought about allowing users to pass an updates dictionary to get_output/get_output_for where layers can register additional updates. That's needed for recurrent layers involving nondeterminism, and we could use it for the BatchNormLayer as well, instead of the default_updates trick. That's something we can change later, though (as long as we change it before releasing Lasagne 0.2).

I haven't really decided what I think of this. It's maybe kind of odd to have to pass in an object to a method that is then modified in-place, since we don't really do this anywhere else. But it is probably the cleanest way to implement this. `default_updates` is a bit magical and could potentially lead to hard-to-track-down bugs. Explicit is better than implicit.

> After training, it's possible to merge mean/std into gamma/beta, to make evaluation a little faster. Shall we add some support for that or leave it up to the user? Actually, gamma/beta can often even be merged into the weights of the previous layer...

I wouldn't prioritize this right now. More of a "nice to have" :) I don't think Lasagne is typically used in use cases where inference speed is critical, and I imagine it doesn't make a huge difference. Does it?

> Not sure how useful this is, but at least it allows to disable maintaining the exponential moving averages if you're not going to use them for some reason

That's valuable I guess.

> A helper function merging all batch normalization layers of a network into their predecessors would be fun.

It would! I've also been thinking of a helper function that merges all model parameters into a single vector (like Torch's getParameters()). Could perhaps be useful when used in combination with scipy.optimize, or other "external" optimizers.

> In hindsight, I think the best solution would have been not to have a nonlinearity keyword in any of the layers, and make the nonlinearities Layer classes as well. That would make it much more straightforward to support parameterizable nonlinearities, and to insert batch normalization between a linear transformation and its nonlinearity. It's too late for that, though.

I think we've had this discussion before, but the main motivation for this is to have the concept of a `Layer` in Lasagne match the concept of a layer as it is understood by most neural network practitioners as closely as possible. That requires making the nonlinearity part of certain `Layer` classes.

> I added a nonlinearity keyword here because usually, you will need a nonlinearity right after the batch normalization, but maybe it should be removed completely? I'd find it confusing to have the BatchNormLayer apply a linear rectifier by default, as that's not really the purpose of batch normalization.

Yeah, I think it's okay to remove it here.
Parametrize with pairs seems fine to me.

> I think the layer will fail if the tensor is not broadcastable but the shapes are. Should we take care of that or is it the users reponsiblity to not use non-broadcastable tensors?

I think this is not always in the user's hands. In `get_output_for()`, we should make dimensions broadcastable that have a size of `1` and need broadcasting.

In the test code from https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/569/files there was a small error, it used  MaxPool2DNNLayer instead of  MaxPool2DDNNLayer. Fixed this aswell. Now I just need to get the squashing right!

> ( I guess the back propagation can't pass through the added dimension).

Well, the added dimension can be seen as an extra, one-sided zero padding of the input, after dilation and ordinary symmetric padding. In your case, it will result in an additional output row and column that depends on the outermost input and outermost filter pixels:
```
Input  abcdefghijklmn
Filter ABC

0a b c d e f g h i j k l m n00  # dilated, zero-padded input plus extra zero
ABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABC  # 9 applications plus extra application
 ABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABC    # 9 applications
  ABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABCABC   # 9 applications
```
So there's nothing wrong with it. The output still defaults to 27 because forward convolution of 27 elements with a 3-element filter, pad=same and stride 2 would be enough to give 14 elements:
```
0abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyza0
111 333 555 777 999 111 333
  222 444 666 888 000 222 444
```

> Is there better way to solve this kind of problem?

No, just a better interface, proposed in #711 and hopefully implemented in #745. Closing this!

Basically I have a Property model with a Money attribute named "Monthly_Price".
The properties may have different currencies configured in their respective monthly_price attribute. 
I'm implementing a View to filter Max Prices for the properties to be displayed in the frontend based on this monthly_price attribute.

My ideal situation would have been to have this queryset Property.objects.filter(monthly_price__lte<Money(1000, 'USD')) and automatically would check all the properties and apply currency conversion in the fly to their monthly_price attribute **without considering only the ones with the same currency.** (as it works right now).

3. Filtering in the view the MoneyManager queryset using the annotated field "monthly_price_usd" using the selected max price in the frontend converted to USD.

Thank you for the proposed change. To make it more precise, you can use any IPython versions prior to IPython 6.

Also, all underlying angr libraries (CLE, archinfo, claripy, etc.) are all Python 3 compatible. So the description that "angr is a suite of Python 2 libraries" might be misleading.

I think the problem is with C++ library support inangr. This challenge binary uses many functions from libstdc++, which is not fully supported by angr. Honestly I have no idea what happens when you executing those functions in angr. It's worth looking into, but getting C++ support in angr is not one of our priorities at the moment.

```
In [5]: pyvex.IRSB('\x89\x46', 0x298d7, archinfo.ArchARMEL(), bytes_offset=1, traceflags=-1)
[15:57:14] [pyvex.lift.libvex]       |     INFO: 
------------------------ Front end ------------------------

        (thumb) 0x298D6:  mov r9, r1
```

You have to do this to specify that there is a discrepancy between the start of the bytes you've provided and the address you've provided. Without it, pyvex will assume that 0x89 is the byte in memory at address 0x298d7. By providing bytes_offset, it will understand that 0x89 is actually the value in memory at address 0x298d6.

But anyway, this version of your yaml format should work better, by using urls rather than indexes. I don't see all too much advantage to yaml over json in this case though...

```yaml
# required
title : my playlist

# required 
song_urls : [
"url0",
"url1",
"url2",
"url3",
"url4",
"url5",
]

# generated on first load
meta:
    "url0":                 # references songs url array
        title  : Song title # doesn't have to match video title
        length : 200        # just for matching purposes

    "url1":
        title  : Song title 2
        length : 205
```

Equivalent json:
```json
{
    "title": "my playlist",

    "song_urls": [
        "url0",
        "url1",
        "url2",
        "url3",
        "url4",
        "url5"
    ],

    "meta": {
        "url1": {
            "length": 205,
            "title": "Song title 2"
        },
        "url0": {
            "length": 200,
            "title": "Song title"
        }
    }
}
```

Oh, as a side note, it could be better to use youtube ids rather than url. Any format meant to be written by the user, though, should handle both and convert as necessary.
